narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 71% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction was based on several factors from your academic and personal profile.

Let's look at your current situation. Your grades in Year 1 and Year 3 placed you in the 5th and 3rd deciles, respectively, which are slightly below the average deciles of 5.71 and 5.53 for other students. Your LSAT score of 31 is also below the average score of 36.70, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 is lower than the average GPA of 3.21. These academic indicators were the most influential in the model's prediction, suggesting that they pushed the prediction toward a higher likelihood of failing the bar exam.

Your family income bracket is higher than average, which typically would be a positive factor, but in this case, it was less influential compared to your academic performance. Additionally, your racial background and full-time work status were considered, but they had a minimal impact on the prediction. Gender was the least influential factor in this decision.

Given these insights, focusing on improving your academic performance could significantly enhance your chances of passing the bar exam in the future. Consider seeking additional support or resources to boost your LSAT score and GPA. By addressing these areas, you can strengthen your application and increase your likelihood of success. Remember, this prediction is not a definitive outcome but rather a guide to help you identify areas for improvement. Keep striving, and don't hesitate to reach out for support as you work toward your goals.
